segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK
title: ONE FILE UPLOAD, A COMPLETE
COURSE APPEARS.
segments.body_html:
Select .imscc→Click Import→Course Structure Built

Forty seconds. No terminal. No API. The course is there.

beats.text: The moment is deceptively simple. A professor opens Canvas Settings, clicks Import Course Content, selects a file called ai-for-designers.imscc received from a colleague, and chooses All Content. For about forty seconds nothing visible happens. Then the import queue updates: Complete. She opens the course and fifteen modules appear in the left navigation. Module 1 is Chapter 1 — The Fluency Trap. She opens it: the chapter text rendered as HTML, below it an exercises assignment shell. The syllabus page renders. She has never opened a terminal. She has not touched the Canvas API. She has not written a line of XML. This is the central counterintuitive fact that this chapter works backward from: how a single properly structured file can instantiate an entire course.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · STAKES
title: UNDERSTAND .IMSCC, UNDERSTAND
COURSE PORTABILITY ITSELF.
segments.body_html:
What portability enablesTeach on Canvas · Archive durably · Migrate to Moodle · Read decades later
⇔
What lock-in costsProprietary formats · Platform dependency · Difficult migration · Durability risk

One standard file format, any 1EdTech-compliant LMS. This is the institutional stakes.

beats.text: The .imscc file is built on IMS Common Cartridge, a standard maintained by 1EdTech, currently at version 1.3. Common Cartridge is what Canvas supports, what Moodle supports, what Blackboard and Brightspace support. A valid Common Cartridge 1.3 package is portable across all of them. That portability is not accidental. It means a course authored once can be deployed to multiple platforms, archived durably in a standards-based format that will remain readable for decades, and migrated away from any single LMS without rebuilding the entire course structure. The alternative — platform-specific formats — creates lock-in, fragility, and institutional dependency. Understanding how the .imscc works is understanding how to build courses that outlast their platforms and remain durable as institutions change.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: A .IMSCC IS SIMPLY A ZIP
WITH XML INDEX.
segments.body_html:
The file.imscc extension signals intent to the LMS ecosystem; structurally it is identical to .zip
Inside the archiveimsmanifest.xml at root directory; web_resources/ folder containing HTML pages
How to verifyRename .imscc to .zip on your operating system and open it as any archive

The format is IMS Common Cartridge 1.3, maintained by 1EdTech, supported by Canvas, Moodle, Blackboard, and Brightspace.

beats.text: The .imscc file is not a proprietary binary. Rename it from .imscc to .zip and your operating system will open it like any other archive. Inside you will find exactly two things: a file called imsmanifest.xml sitting at the root, and a folder called web_resources/ containing HTML pages. That is the whole package. The XML file is the index; the web_resources folder holds the content. The .imscc extension is simply the file-type marker the LMS ecosystem agreed on to distinguish a Common Cartridge from a generic ZIP archive. Structurally, they are identical. The extension signals intent — this is a course package, not just a random collection of files. The format specification, IMS Common Cartridge version 1.3, is maintained by 1EdTech. Canvas, Moodle, Blackboard, and Brightspace all support it.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE MANIFEST DECLARES THREE THINGS
SIMULTANEOUSLY.
segments.body_html:
MetadataVersion number (1.3) and course title
OrganizationModule hierarchy: items and child items
ResourcesFlat list: every file with ID, type, and path

imsmanifest.xml is what makes the package a course rather than a loose folder of HTML pages.

beats.text: The imsmanifest.xml file tells the LMS three things simultaneously, each essential. First, metadata: what version of the Common Cartridge spec this package targets, and what the course is called. This is the header — the LMS reads it to confirm compatibility and display the course title. Second, the organization: the module hierarchy. Each module is an item element. Each content item inside the module is a child item that points to a resource by its identifier. This is where the fifteen modules come from in the opening example — the manifest declares them, and Canvas reads the declaration to build the left-navigation structure. Third, the resources: a flat list of every file the manifest references. Each resource gets an identifier, a type, and an href pointing to its location in the web_resources folder. This three-part structure is the contract between the file and the LMS.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE ORGANIZATION LAYER BUILDS
NAVIGATION HIERARCHY.
segments.body_html:
Module (item)→Child items (content pages)→Resource IDs

Each item element points to resources by identifier. Canvas reads this tree and renders it as the left-navigation sidebar.

beats.text: The organization section of the manifest is a tree structure. At the top level, each module is an item element with a label and an identifier. Inside that item, child items represent the content pieces of the module — chapter pages, exercise shells, discussion prompts. Each child item has a reference to a resource by identifier. Canvas reads this entire tree when it imports the package. It reconstructs the left-navigation sidebar from the item hierarchy. The module labels become the sidebar section headers. The child items become the content links within each section. The resource identifiers tell Canvas where to find the actual HTML files in the web_resources folder. This design separates structure from content, which is why you can reorder modules by editing the manifest without moving files.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: RESOURCES MUST BE DECLARED
FOR THE LMS TO FIND THEM.
segments.body_html:
The resource contract: If the manifest references a resource that is not in the ZIP, the import will error or skip. If the ZIP contains a file the manifest does not reference, that file is invisible to the LMS — it sits in the archive but the course structure does not know about it.

Every HTML file needs both a resource entry and a reference in the organization hierarchy.

beats.text: The resources section of the manifest is a flat list. Every file in the web_resources folder that should appear in the course must be declared here with a unique identifier, a type describing what kind of content it is, and an href pointing to its path in the ZIP archive. This creates the essential contract: the manifest must be synchronized with the actual files. If the manifest references a resource that does not exist in web_resources, Canvas will either skip the reference or report an error during import — the course will be incomplete. Conversely, if the ZIP contains an HTML file that is not listed in the manifest, that file exists in the archive but is unreachable to the LMS. It is invisible to the course structure. The manifest is the authority; the files are the content. Without the manifest declaring them, the files are merely data, not part of the course.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE MANIFEST IS A PACKING LIST
FOR THE MOVING BOX.
segments.body_html:
The HTML filesObjects in the moving box — chapter content, exercises, assets
The manifestThe packing list — tells the LMS where everything is and how it fits

Without the packing list, the LMS cannot reconstruct the course. Without the objects, the packing list references things that do not exist.

beats.text: Think of the .imscc as a moving box. The HTML files in web_resources are the objects you are moving — the content that makes up the course. The imsmanifest.xml is the packing list taped to the outside. When the LMS receives the package, it opens the packing list first. The list tells it: there are fifteen modules, here are their names and order, here are the content pages inside each module, here is where each file is located in the box. The LMS then pulls each object from the box according to the list and puts it in the right place in the course structure. If the packing list says there is a chapter-1.html file but the file is not there, the import fails or leaves a gap. If the box contains a chapter-16.html file but the packing list does not mention it, the file is invisible — it is in the archive but not part of the course. This separation of manifest from content is elegant because it means you can reorganize an entire course by editing the manifest without touching the HTML files.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: CANVAS-FLAVORED ADDS RICHNESS BUT
NOT PORTABILITY.
segments.body_html:
Standard Common CartridgeModules, pages, assessments, discussions, manifest. Clean import everywhere.
Canvas-flavored CCStandard + syllabus.html + course_settings.xml. Canvas-specific features. Not portable.

Canvas reads two kinds of Common Cartridge. The difference is two optional files.

beats.text: Canvas supports two different kinds of Common Cartridge packages, and the difference matters profoundly. A standard Common Cartridge — what the 1EdTech specification defines — contains modules, web content pages, assessment items, discussion shells, and the manifest. It imports cleanly into Canvas and into any other 1EdTech-compliant LMS. It is portable and unambiguous. A Canvas-flavored Common Cartridge contains everything in the standard package plus two trigger files: course_settings/syllabus.html and course_settings/course_settings.xml. When Canvas sees those files, it reads them as course-level configuration — populating the syllabus page with the HTML content, applying course navigation settings, potentially setting module completion requirements and rubric structures through Canvas-specific XML that other LMS platforms do not recognize. The two trigger files are the boundary between the portable path and the Canvas-specific path.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE TRIGGER FILES MARK THE BOUNDARY
BETWEEN PORTABLE AND PROPRIETARY.
segments.body_html:
course_settings/syllabus.htmlCanvas reads this as the course syllabus; other LMS platforms ignore it
course_settings/course_settings.xmlCanvas-specific configuration: completion requirements, navigation, outcomes
The choiceInclude these files: rich Canvas integration, not portable. Omit them: clean standard import everywhere

The choice between standard and Canvas-optimized is a choice between compatibility and richness.

beats.text: Canvas-flavored Common Cartridge adds two files in a course_settings folder. The syllabus.html file gets read by Canvas and displayed on the course syllabus page; other LMS platforms either ignore it or try to parse it as a generic webpage, producing odd results. The course_settings.xml file contains Canvas-specific metadata about module completion requirements, rubric structures, learning outcome alignments, and navigation settings — all features that Canvas supports but that the 1EdTech standard does not define, so other platforms cannot read them. A Canvas-optimized package imports richly into Canvas and poorly into everywhere else. A standard package imports cleanly into all platforms but leaves the syllabus blank and the advanced features unconfigured. The choice between the two paths is a choice between compatibility and richness. You can be optimized for one platform or portable to all platforms. You cannot be both.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THIS CHAPTER CHOOSES STANDARD —
THE DURABLE PATH.
segments.body_html:
Design philosophy: A standard package that imports cleanly into Canvas, Moodle, Blackboard, and Brightspace is more durable than a Canvas-optimized package that imports richly into Canvas and fails elsewhere.

build-imscc-standard.py deliberately does not write the Canvas-specific trigger files. This is intentional.

beats.text: This chapter teaches the standard path: pure Python, no external dependencies, portable across platforms. The script build-imscc-standard.py does not write the Canvas-specific trigger files. That is not an oversight. The reasoning is durability. An institution that invests in a Canvas-optimized Common Cartridge package locks itself into Canvas or accepts that migration to another platform will require manual rebuilding. An institution that invests in a standard Common Cartridge package can teach on Canvas, archive to institutional storage in a portable format, migrate to Moodle or Blackboard without losing the structure, and read the course a decade from now even if Canvas no longer exists. The standard path is simpler, requires no external Ruby toolchain, and creates an artifact that outlasts any single platform.

segments.label: 11 / 12 · SYNTHESIS
title: ONE FILE, ANY LMS —
IF YOU CHOOSE PORTABILITY.
segments.body_html:
The central insight is that a single properly structured file — a ZIP archive with a manifest and HTML pages — can instantiate an entire course across any 1EdTech-compliant LMS. The choice to omit Canvas-specific trigger files is a deliberate design decision: durability over platform optimization.

Understanding how the .imscc works is understanding how to write courses that outlast their platforms and remain readable, editable, and teachable decades from now.

beats.text: Here is what this chapter has built toward: the .imscc file is a contract between a course source and any learning management system that reads the Common Cartridge standard. The contract has three parts. First, the manifest structure — metadata, organization, resources — tells the LMS how to rebuild the course. Second, the separation between structure and content means you can reorganize the entire course by editing XML without touching the files. Third, the choice to omit Canvas-specific trigger files means the package will import cleanly into Canvas, Moodle, Blackboard, and Brightspace. This third choice embodies the philosophy underlying the chapter: durability matters more than richness for institutional content. A professor who archives a course in standard Common Cartridge format today can rest assured that in twenty years, whether the institution still uses Canvas or has migrated elsewhere, someone will still be able to read, edit, and teach from that course. That is what understanding the .imscc buys you.

segments.label: 12 / 12 · CLOSE
segments.body_html:
COURSE EXPORT//IMS COMMON CARTRIDGE//PORTABILITY
